danger_signals.visual_form: Grotesque enemy sprite design with exaggerated features (e.g., Major Mucus as alien made of snot, Queen Slug-for-a-Butt with gastropod mollusc)
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile entities that must be avoided or defeated

danger_signals.visual_form: Pits, obstacles, asteroids in gameplay sections
danger_signals.meaning: Level-based threats requiring platforming skill to navigate

danger_signals.visual_form: Large, visually distinct villain sprites at level culmination (Hickboy, Psy-Crow, Evil the Cat, Bob the Killer Goldfish, Professor Monkey-for-a-Head)
danger_signals.meaning: Critical combat encounter requiring defeat to progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Computer-controlled character with health status visible during 'For Pete's Sake' level
danger_signals.meaning: Escort mission danger—player failure to protect results in health depletion via Peter's lashing out

danger_signals.visual_form: Underwater maze sections with time limit mechanic
danger_signals.meaning: Urgency and failure state if time expires

safety_signals.visual_form: Transition to next level or 'Andy Asteroids' racing section
safety_signals.meaning: Player has navigated hazards and is advancing

safety_signals.visual_form: Item pickups during 'Andy Asteroids' racing section (boosts, collectibles)
safety_signals.meaning: Resource acquisition for survival and success in races

safety_signals.visual_form: Jim's worm body acting as functional whip, hooks for grappling
safety_signals.meaning: Player tool for both offense and environmental traversal (safe crossing via swinging)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Jim is the target of all antagonists seeking to reclaim his suit
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Jim is the only worm-in-suit character; visually distinct from all other entities

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Grotesquely designed, anatomically bizarre creatures (snot aliens, monkey-headed professors, slug-bottomed queens)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each boss has unique design and abilities; variety suggests loose alliance of diverse villains rather than unified faction
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout levels as obstacles and boss encounters; no unified territory or control scheme evident

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Recurring antagonist appearing in 'Andy Asteroids' racing sections and as boss encounter
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Primary rival character with dedicated racing mechanic
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appears in between-level racing sections, establishing persistent opposition

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Not antagonistic; narrative goal that Jim must reach

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level design transitions from starting area through themed environments to boss encounters; visual complexity and enemy variety increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy patterns become more aggressive; boss encounters present unique mechanics; racing sections interrupt traditional platforming

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Jim always appears in robotic suit; suit is essential to human-like operation and combat capability
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Without suit, Jim would be a normal earthworm; suit enables gunplay, whipping, and platforming abilities

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: In 'For Pete's Sake' level, Peter is visible as computer-controlled companion with health/status implications
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must actively protect Peter by whipping him over pits and clearing enemies before they reach him; failure results in immediate health damage to Jim

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Camera shifts to behind-Jim perspective in 'Andy Asteroids' sections; tube-like structure and asteroid field environment
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Victory in race advances game; loss triggers mandatory Psy-Crow boss fight before progression allowed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Underwater maze sections with spatial-obstacle design; bungee-jumping competitive sections with fighting mechanics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different level types require different skill applications; crash limits in underwater sections; combat-platforming hybrid in bungee sections

environmental_storytelling.example: Professor Monkey-for-a-Head and Queen Slug-for-a-Butt communicate absurdist, grotesque world through visual design alone; world is ruled by bizarre, unnatural authority figures
Super suit falling from sky establishes supernatural/sci-fi element; Jim's transformation from normal earthworm to powered protagonist told through visual change alone
Different themed levels (New Junk City visible in screenshots) suggest a world of diverse, dangerous locations Jim must traverse to reach Princess
Collection of visually unique antagonists (goldfish, organs, vomiting junkyard men) implies chaotic world without single unifying threat; each area has its own guardian
Deliberately grotesque enemy designs serve as satire of platform game conventions; the game's unique atmosphere was intentional rejection of licensed-game restrictions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Platforming, shooting, whipping enemies; can swing from hooks; vulnerable to enemy contact
Rocket-based movement; avoids asteroids; collects boosts and items; races against Psy-Crow
Must actively whip Peter over pits; must defeat enemies before they reach Peter; takes health damage if Peter is hit
Competes in 'Andy Asteroids' sections; if wins race, triggers mandatory boss encounter
Attacks Jim with combat mechanics; must be defeated to progress
Computer-controlled; requires player whip interaction to jump over obstacles; attacks Jim if harmed
Present in levels as obstacles; can be avoided or defeated; threaten both Jim and escort targets
Each boss has unique attack patterns and visual animations; appear at level culmination; must be defeated for progression

notes: Earthworm Jim communicates world-state primarily through grotesque visual design and character archetypes rather than traditional faction systems. The game is deliberately absurdist and satirical, with the visual communication serving as narrative commentary on platform game conventions. The world features no unified political faction—instead, it's populated by bizarre, independent antagonists each representing a different threat archetype. State changes are signaled through gameplay mechanic shifts (racing vs. platforming vs. escort) and visual environment transitions. The suit is the central visual and mechanical anchor; Jim's identity is inseparable from it. Danger is communicated through grotesque enemy design, environmental hazards, and boss encounter framing. No traditional 'safe zones' exist—progression is continuous, with each level transition representing a new threat. The satire-first design approach means visual communication prioritizes tone and absurdity over clarity.
sources_used: tcrf - Earthworm Jim (SNES) - The Cutting Room Floor
